Name: Multi-dimensional Array (Tensor) Primitive with Manual Striding
Summary: This skill covers the design and implementation of a fundamental multi-dimensional array (tensor) data structure using raw memory pointers and manual stride calculation. It demonstrates how to manage memory views for N-dimensional data stored in a flat buffer, specifically focusing on row-major layout, to enable efficient element access without relying on external numerical libraries. This is foundational for building custom computation kernels or minimal inference engines.
Usage: To apply this skill, one would implement a `Tensor` (or similar) class using a raw pointer for data. The core involves correctly calculating `strides` during construction for a chosen memory layout (e.g., row-major). The `at()` method efficiently translates multi-dimensional indices into a single flat buffer offset using these pre-computed strides. This primitive can then be extended with fundamental operations like element-wise addition, matrix multiplication, etc., by defining how they interact with the underlying memory buffer based on the tensor's shape and strides. This approach is valuable for performance-critical applications or when deep control over memory layout is required.
author: anonymous
